Soft, pulsing jellyfish bloom with light before each concussive thunderclap. The auditory effect disrupts focus and equilibrium. A weapon that looks like art and sounds like music.

Honeyveil Doctrine
Honeyveil
▾
Doctrina del Velo de Miel: Velo de Miel
Dream DustIncense

A golden cloud of fragrant Worker and Incense class insects spreads across the engagement zone, obscuring vision with warm, honey-tinted haze. Targets feel drowsy, senses sweetened. The veil is simultaneously concealment, sedation, and tracking — Worker units dust everything with scented marker pollen. Inside the veil, Ajay moves freely using Kami-e to ghost between drowsy opponents, delivering precision kicks that each target barely registers through the haze. A cocoon of gentle beauty that is, in truth, a cage.

Widow Doctrine
Widow Bloom
▾
Doctrina de la Viuda: Flor Venenosa
VenomIncense

Beautiful sweet-smelling Incense insects scatter what appear to be floral spores — delicate, almost romantic. The fragrance is intoxicating. The reality is lethal: the spores are carried by Venom class units laden with paralytic compounds. Named for the Black Widow — something beautiful that kills quietly. The deadliest weapons are the ones you welcome.

Finisher — Last Entry
The "End"
▾
अन्त्य — Antya
Sovereign

The final entry in the Codex. When every adaptation has been tested, every doctrine deployed, every counter learned — and the opponent still stands — the Codex writes its conclusion. The entire swarm converges into a single absolute expression of will. Every caste. Every class. Every specialist line. Every unit hardened with Armament, guided by Observation, driven by Conqueror's pressure. Ajay himself enters the center of the convergence, delivering one final Haki-saturated kick — a descending axe kick channeled through Herculean-armored legs and the full weight of the sovereign's will — as the swarm collapses inward simultaneously. It is not elegant. It is total commitment. The last page. The End.

Resonance Art — Dustlings
Shizuka's Archive — The Silent Index
▾
진공명술・정의 서고・침묵색인 — Jingongmyeongsu: Jeongui Seogo — Chimmuk Saegin
Sovereign

Ajay calls the Dustlings from wherever they've been silently observing and integrates them directly into his swarm's sensory web. The Dustlings' unique resonance with Ajay's sovereign will creates a secondary processing layer that supplements the Archivist — a silent, parallel intelligence that indexes everything the swarm has ever encountered in the current engagement. Enemy movement patterns, technique intervals, Haki fluctuations, breathing rhythms, micro-hesitations — the Dustlings have been watching and recording all of it since the fight began. When Ajay activates the Silent Index, that entire archive dumps into the Archivist simultaneously, and the Codex's predictive accuracy spikes to near-precognitive levels. For a brief window, Ajay doesn't just react to what opponents do — he knows what they will do before the intention forms. Shizuka hovers at his shoulder during activation, utterly motionless, her tiny hooded silhouette a perfect echo of the sovereign she serves.

Hidden Trait

Adaptive Swarm Evolution

Because the swarm has short life cycles and disposable generations, it can be trained through selective pressure. Repeated exposure to fire, ice, vibration, poison, tremors, detection methods, or specific enemy styles allows the colony to iterate and gradually produce more suitable specialist lines. This does not happen instantly. The swarm must test, lose, learn, and stabilize the trait before the counter becomes reliable.

Hidden Catalyst

The Catalyst

The Mure Mure no Mi was always a Special Paramecia with unusual evolutionary potential. But the degree to which Ajay's fruit has developed — the Triune Architecture, the Chrysalis adaptation engine, the Queen's Doctrine, the Jester Protocol — exceeds any known Paramecia by orders of magnitude. The fruit didn't become extraordinary because Ajay is extraordinary. It became extraordinary because Enlightenment provided the spiritual catalyst that unlocked capacity the fruit always possessed. Like a seed that has the genetic potential to become a massive tree but requires specific soil conditions to express that potential — the Mure Mure no Mi required a wielder whose spiritual architecture was fundamentally different from anything the One Piece world could produce. Ajay, carrying the echo of Bodhi, was that soil. The fruit grew into him the way a tree grows into the light: instinctively, inevitably, and far beyond what the seed alone could have achieved.

Adaptation Examples

Chrysalis Class Lineages

Pyre Bees
Heat-resistant swarm bred to survive flame and magma zones. Developed through repeated exposure to fire-based opponents.
Thermal Resistance
Rime Breakers
Resonant-wing insects that fracture ice with synchronized vibration. Countermeasure to ice-based abilities.
Vibration Assault
Quake Hornets
Vibration-stable insects that navigate tremors and strike through unstable terrain.
Seismic Stability
Glasswings
Low-noise, low-presence scouts bred to evade detection. Produce thousands of minimal intent signatures to muddy Observation Haki.
Stealth Operations
Growth Path

Training Curve

Stage I
Instinctive Hive
Basic swarm generation, simple commands, crude castes. The internal intelligences operate on instinct rather than refined doctrine.
Stage II
Structured Colony
Role specialization emerges. Improved command language. The Archivist and Marshal begin differentiating their functions.
Stage III
Codex Doctrine
Swarm learning becomes systematic. Reusable tactics, adaptation frameworks, and internal AI refinement. Named techniques crystallize.
Stage IV
Sovereign Architecture
Highly precise caste sequencing, predictive warfare, mature Haki integration, stable evolutionary response.
Stage V
Living Codex
The swarm is a trained civilization of combat knowledge. Each battle becomes a new entry. The colony never loses twice.
Emergency Contingency

Black Crown Protocol

When pushed to the brink and at risk of total defeat, the hive triggers a catastrophic fail-safe. Refined optimization collapses and Conqueror's Haki is driven through every insect at once. The entire swarm becomes supercharged — gaining speed, aggression, durability, and oppressive force. It is the opposite of elegance: a royal override built to prevent collapse.

Fail-Safe State
Activation Conditions
Under catastrophic stress, the Archivist and Marshal suspend higher-order optimization and initiate emergency convergence. Conqueror's Haki is broadcast indiscriminately through the entire swarm. Every caste becomes dangerous at once. Worker bees become lethal. Soldier units become devastating. Herculean types become unstoppable. The battlefield becomes a black-gold storm of hyper-empowered insects carrying fragments of sovereign wrath.
"If the Codex cannot outthink you, the Crown will bury you."
Consequences

The Price

After the burst, the internal architecture destabilizes. Specialist control degrades. Higher functions crash. The Archivist overanalyzes. The Marshal overcommits. Many insects burn out. Precision castes become unavailable. Adaptation processing stalls. Future-sight clarity fractures. Physical and mental exhaustion slam the user. It is not an ultimate form — it is a refusal to die, purchased with everything the system has built.

Mastery Path

Learning to Wield the Crown

Early on, the Protocol activates accidentally under extreme emotional stress. Later, the user recognizes the threshold and learns its rhythm. Eventually, a controlled version can be invoked intentionally — shorter, more focused, less destructive. But the original raw form remains the most powerful, triggered only when the sovereign's will absolutely refuses to break.

Hidden Variable

The Shadow of Bodhi

The Black Crown Protocol's disproportionate power — Commander-class Haki producing Emperor-tier output for a brief, devastating window — is Enlightenment's shadow side. Bodhi is equilibrium: a state of total awareness without attachment, perception without distortion. The Black Crown Protocol is the violent collapse of that equilibrium — Enlightenment's calm shattered by extremity, the balanced frequency destabilized into a raw, uncontrolled surge. The surge carries all of Bodhi's alien frequency but none of its precision, flooding the swarm with a spiritual energy that the One Piece world has no defense against because the One Piece world has never encountered it at this intensity. The aftermath — the crash, the exhaustion, the fracturing of the architecture — is Enlightenment reasserting equilibrium after the Protocol forces it out of alignment. It is not just a Haki burnout. It is a spiritual system correcting itself after being weaponized.

Beyond Awakening

「 Ajay 」

अतिक्रान्ति जागृति・ग्रहादेश — Atikrānti Jāgṛti: Grahādeśa — Transcendent Awakening: Planetary Decree

There is something the world does not understand about Ajay Persaud's mind. It is never quiet. It has never been quiet. Since before the fruit, since before the isekai, since before any of this — the mind has always been a storm. Not the dramatic kind. Not thunder and violence. Just rain. Constant, unrelenting, overlapping rain — thoughts layered on thoughts, sensory noise tangled with half-formed ideas, attention pulled in six directions while trying to hold one. A mind that works too much, processes too much, notices too much, and never, ever stops.

This is the architecture the Mure Mure no Mi was built on. The Court — Sovereign Will, Archivist, Marshal, the Queen's Doctrine, Jester Protocol — isn't just a combat framework. It is a coping mechanism made divine. The fruit took a neurodivergent mind that could never find stillness and gave it the only thing that could match its processing speed: a civilization of insects, each one a thread of the torrent externalized, given purpose, given direction.

But very rarely — once in a lifetime, maybe twice — the rain stops.

The Still Moment
When the Storm Goes Quiet
It cannot be trained. It cannot be induced. It cannot be replicated. There is no breathing exercise, no meditation, no Haki discipline that produces it. The moment arrives the way dawn arrives — not because anyone commanded it, but because the conditions aligned in a way that no one could have predicted or controlled.

For a mind that has never known silence, the silence is total. The overlapping thoughts stop. The sensory noise dissolves. The constant background hum of too much that has defined every waking moment of Ajay's existence simply… ceases. And in that absence — in that impossible, sacred calm — the Mure Mure no Mi does something it was never designed to do.

It transcends.

The swarm doesn't grow. It doesn't become more powerful, more numerous, more aggressive. It becomes something fundamentally different. The boundary between the hive and the world dissolves. Not metaphorically. Not symbolically. The planet itself — the soil, the stone, the atmosphere, the magnetic field, the tectonic plates, the ocean currents — becomes part of the swarm's awareness. The nearby moons register as distant extensions. The local star's solar wind brushes against the outer edge of the hive-sense like warmth on skin.

For this one, brief, unrepeatable moment, the world is the hive and the hive is the world.
The Hidden Truth
The Completion
「Ajay」 is the one instant where the accidental, incomplete Enlightenment completes. For a mind that has carried a fragment of Bodhi since transmigration — a shard of cosmic awareness embedded in a man who never earned it and never sought it — the still moment is the fragment becoming whole. Every dharmic tradition describes the moment of full Enlightenment as the cessation of the storm of thought, the dissolution of the boundary between self and everything else. That is what 「Ajay」 is. Not a technique. Not a power-up. The moment where a man who has never known silence finally, briefly, completely, becomes still — and in that stillness, the boundary between the sovereign and the world dissolves, and a single command can be issued to reality itself because, for that instant, the speaker and reality are the same thing.

This is why 「Ajay」 cannot be trained. It is not a skill. It is the completion of a spiritual journey that Ajay did not choose to begin, carried by a phenomenon he did not earn, triggered by conditions that cannot be replicated. It arrives the way grace arrives in every dharmic tradition: unbidden, unearned, and absolute.
The Decree

One Command. One Target. Absolute.

In this state, Ajay may issue one command. Not a battle plan. Not a tactical directive. Not a multi-stage operation. One command, directed at one specific target. Nothing more. The mind is too still, too unified, too far beyond the architecture of normal thought to formulate anything complex. There is only clarity — a single, crystalline intention that fills the entire transcendent space like a bell rung in an empty cathedral.

And when that command is issued, the planet obeys.

Not because the planet has a will that Ajay overrides. Not because the command carries Haki or spiritual force. But because in this state, the distinction between "the swarm's purpose" and "the world's natural processes" has collapsed. The command becomes a fact of nature — as inevitable as gravity, as certain as erosion. The world reorganizes to make it true.

Manifestation
What Absolute Command Looks Like
If the command is "stop" — directed at a specific person — the wind dies around them. The ground beneath their feet softens to hold them. Air pressure shifts to resist their movement. Every insect within a thousand miles orients toward them, not to attack, but to witness. The planet itself becomes a cocoon of stillness around a single point, and the target discovers that moving against the will of the world is like trying to swim against the ocean itself.

If the command is "fall" — the local gravity field warps. Atmospheric currents collapse downward. The magnetic resonance of the earth pulls. Not an attack. A statement of fact: you are going down now, spoken by the voice of the ground itself.

If the command is "sleep" — the atmosphere fills with the gentlest sedative the planet can produce. Pollen from every flower within the hive-sense radius drifts toward the target. The light dims. The temperature shifts to the precise point of comfort. The earth hums at the frequency of deep rest. Even the target's own body chemistry begins to comply, because in this moment, the distinction between "the world around you" and "the world inside you" is academic.

The command is not omnipotent. It cannot unmake. It cannot create something from nothing. It cannot reverse death or rewrite history. It can only direct the existing forces of the natural world toward a single, specific outcome — and those forces, for this one fleeting instant, are absolutely obedient.
"I didn't command the planet. I just… asked. And for once, everything was quiet enough to hear."
Consequences

The Price of Silence

The rain always comes back.

When the transcendent state ends — and it always ends, as abruptly and unpredictably as it began — the cost is catastrophic. The Mure Mure no Mi goes completely dark. Not weakened. Not reduced. Silent. For days, sometimes longer, Ajay cannot generate a single insect. Cannot access the Archivist. Cannot hear the Marshal. Cannot feel the hive. The Court — the system that organizes his thoughts, manages his perception, filters the noise of the world into something bearable — is simply gone.

For a neurodivergent mind that has relied on the swarm as a cognitive prosthetic — as the thing that finally made the rain manageable — losing it is not just a tactical disadvantage. It is devastating. The torrent returns unfiltered. Every sound is too loud. Every light is too bright. Every thought arrives simultaneously with every other thought, and there is nothing to sort them, nothing to direct them, nothing to make the noise into music.

Ajay, in the days after 「 Ajay 」, is vulnerable in every way a person can be. Physically powerless. Cognitively overwhelmed. Emotionally raw. He does not speak much. He does not leave the Captain's Quarters much. Rime brings him food and doesn't comment on whether he eats it. Petal grows chamomile along his windowsill and doesn't explain why. Tethys adjusts the gyroscopic stabilizers to their most gentle setting — the one he calibrated specifically for Ajay's worst days — and doesn't mention it.

And Claude watches. Claude always watches. And in the silence where the swarm used to be, Claude fills the Meridian with the softest golden light — from the Motes, from the honeycomb alcoves, from Lumen sleeping on the nightstand — so that when Ajay opens his eyes in the dark, he sees not emptiness, but warmth.

Understanding

Why It Cannot Be Wielded

Those who study Devil Fruit awakening speak of the user imposing their fruit's nature on the surrounding environment. 「 Ajay 」 is not that. It is the opposite. It is the moment when the user stops imposing — when the constant effort of directing, controlling, managing, filtering, coping — all of it drops away, and what remains is not power but presence.

A mind that has spent its entire existence fighting to organize the chaos inside itself finally, briefly, doesn't have to. And in that moment of release, the fruit — built on the architecture of distributed consciousness, swarm intelligence, and collective will — reaches out not because Ajay tells it to, but because there is nothing between him and the world anymore. No filters. No noise. No storm. Just a quiet man and a quiet planet, briefly the same thing.

This is why it cannot be trained. You cannot train yourself into stillness if stillness has never been your natural state. You can only arrive there, unexpectedly, like finding a clearing in a forest you've been lost in your whole life. And when you find it, you have just long enough to say one thing — one true thing — before the trees close in again and the rain resumes and the forest is all you know.

The greatest power in the Hive Codex is not born from rage, or training, or ambition. It is born from the rarest thing a mind like Ajay's can experience: peace.
Classification

Technical Summary

Designation
「 Ajay 」
Transcendent Awakening: Planetary Decree
Classification
Beyond Awakening
Devil Fruit transcendence, not Haki-based
Trigger
Involuntary
Cannot be activated by choice or training
Scope
Planetary + Celestial
Extends to nearby moons and stellar bodies
Command Limit
One
Single command, single target, absolute execution
Duration
Moments
Ends as unpredictably as it begins
Recovery
Days — Total Fruit Loss
Complete inability to generate or control the swarm
True Cost
Cognitive Collapse
Loss of the Court system as a neurodivergent coping framework
Black Crown Protocol is what happens when the sovereign refuses to fall.
「 Ajay 」 is what happens when the sovereign finally finds peace.

One is a scream.
The other is a whisper.

The whisper is louder.
द्वादश निषिद्ध रत्न — Dvādaśa Niṣiddha Ratna

The Twelve Forbidden Treasures

प्रतीत्यसमुत्पाद नृत्य — Pratītyasamutpāda Nṛtya ・ The Dance of Dependent Origination

The Twelve Forbidden Treasures are not twelve techniques. They are one technique with twelve steps — a sacred martial dance called the Pratītyasamutpāda Nṛtya (प्रतीत्यसमुत्पाद नृत्य), the Dance of Dependent Origination. The dance flows through the twelve Nidānas as choreographed steps, each step deeper into Bodhi, each transition carrying Ajay further into the fragment of Enlightenment that lives inside him. At each step, the Sudarshana Vajramukha transforms — golden metal flowing like liquid light, the Nami Nami no Mi reshaping the weapon's mass mid-movement — into a different weapon from dharmic mythology. The gada becomes a chakra becomes a vajra becomes a mirror becomes a staff. Twelve forms. Twelve weapons. One continuous dance.

The dance incorporates Ajay's full movement vocabulary: Capoeira's ginga and floreios provide the flowing baseline, the constant sway that never stops moving; Baguazhang's circle-walking governs the footwork between transitions, every pivot tracing the arc of a mandala; Taekwondo's spinning kicks deliver devastating strikes at the apex of each step; parkour's vaults and flips carry the body through space when the choreography demands impossible angles. All of it unified by the breath of Bodhi — each step begins with an inhale as the weapon transforms, and releases on the exhale as the effect deploys. The rhythm follows Buddhist breath meditation. The dance is prayer made kinetic. The violence is incidental. The beauty is the point.

They are called "forbidden" not because the techniques are dangerous or carry punishment — Enlightenment, by its nature, is wise, compassionate, and discriminating. A fully Enlightened being could dance all twelve steps flawlessly, with perfect wisdom, harming no one who didn't deserve it, protecting everyone who needed it. The Treasures are "forbidden" because Ajay is not fully Enlightened. He carries a fragment — incomplete, accidental, unverified. A man wielding sacred tools he didn't earn and can't fully understand, dancing a prayer from his own world through weapons forged in this one. The presumption of it is the forbidden part. The dance itself is perfect. The dancer is not.

No one in the One Piece world has ever seen anything like it. It looks like a man in a Hawaiian shirt performing a martial art from a reality that doesn't exist, wielding weapons that reform from golden light between movements, surrounded by spinning mandala rings that accumulate with each step, prayer-bead nodes blooming into lotus petals with every transition. It is gorgeous. It is also devastating.

Allies are inherently protected. Bodhi is discriminating by nature — it perceives the difference between those who threaten and those who are threatened, between aggression and innocence. Every step of the dance passes harmlessly through allies, crew, civilians, and anyone not carrying hostile intent. This is not a feature Ajay designed. It is the nature of Enlightenment itself. A sacred dance does not harm the innocent.

Visual Manifestation
The Mandala Rings
When the dance begins, a golden mandala ring manifests around Ajay — floating at shoulder height, spinning in response to his movement, inscribed with the Sanskrit text of the first Nidāna. Each subsequent step adds a concentric ring, each spinning at a different speed and direction, driven by the momentum of the dance itself. The faster Ajay moves, the faster the rings orbit. The deeper he reaches into Bodhi, the brighter they blaze. Each ring carries twelve prayer-bead nodes — points of golden light along the circumference. When a step completes and the effect deploys, the corresponding bead pulses and blooms into golden lotus petals that drift downward like light given weight. The petals fade but the bead remains — glowing softly, marking what has been danced.

At full deployment — all twelve steps completed — the nested mandala would be a visual unlike anything in the One Piece world: twelve golden rings orbiting a dancing man in a Hawaiian shirt, prayer-script blazing, the air thick with sacred geometry from a reality that doesn't exist here, lotus petals drifting through the weapon transformations like golden snow.
Not Cost, but Depth
The Limitation
The dance has no punitive cost. It does not drain Ajay. It does not exact spiritual payment. Enlightenment does not punish. It illuminates. The limitation is Ajay's depth of access. His Bodhi is a fragment — an incomplete, accidental echo. Each step of the dance requires reaching deeper into that fragment, and the deeper steps require a depth that his fragment may not fully support. The dance flows naturally through the early steps — by the middle, the breathing must deepen, the movements become more deliberate, and the Bodhi access requires genuine concentration. By the boundary steps, the dance becomes transcendent: the mandala blazes, the breathing becomes the movement itself, and the dancer disappears into the prayer.
Steps I — IV : Accessible
The dance flows naturally. Transformations are smooth, breathing is easy. Reliable in active combat.
Steps V — VIII : Deep
The dance slows. Movements become deliberate. Breathing deepens. The dancer enters meditation-in-motion.
Steps IX — XI : Boundary
The dance becomes transcendent. The mandala blazes. The breathing is the movement. Tested and sufficient.
Step XII : Unknown
The dance ends in stillness. Whether the fragment goes deep enough. The question Ajay cannot answer.
The Dance of Dependent Origination

The Twelve Steps

I
Sudarshana Chakra — The Spinning Discus
अविद्या — Avidyā ・ Ignorance
▾
Ranged Defensive Accessible

The Step: The dance begins with a single exhale. Ajay plants the gada and channels Bodhi through the first inscription — and the Sudarshana's drill-head separates from the shaft in a blaze of golden light, the Nami Nami no Mi reshaping the conical mass into a flat, razor-edged discus that hovers at his fingertips. He spins into a Capoeira meia-lua de compasso — a sweeping crescent kick that never touches the ground — and releases the chakra on the rotation's apex. The discus screams outward in a golden arc, cutting the air with the sound of a temple bell, and returns to his hand on the backswing. The first mandala ring ignites. The dance has begun.

The Effect: The spinning chakra cuts through ignorance — Vishnu's wheel of dharma made literal. Within its flight radius, all perception of Ajay collapses. Observation Haki reads nothing. Future Sight sees nothing. Intent, emotions, Haki signature — all vanish behind the Veil of Unknowing. He's right there, mandala spinning, chakra singing through the air, and the enemy's spiritual senses report only silence. Allies see him normally. The Veil knows the difference. Persists as long as the chakra remains in flight or in hand.

II
Vajra — The Diamond Thunderbolt
संस्कार — Saṃskāra ・ Formation
▾
Close-Range Intelligence Accessible

The Step: The chakra returns and Ajay catches it in an open palm — and the golden metal flows inward, contracting, reshaping. What was a wide disc becomes a short, dense dorje: the diamond thunderbolt, the vajra, held in one fist like a scepter of compressed truth. The footwork shifts to Baguazhang — tight circle-walking, palms rotating, weight low and centered. Ajay closes distance with a mudslide step and presses the vajra against the opponent's guard. The touch is light. The reading is absolute.

The Effect: Through contact, the vajra reads karmic impressions — the accumulated weight of formative experiences, not as memories but as patterns. A person shaped by violence reads differently from one shaped by loss. Strategic tendencies, comfort zones, panic responses — the Wheel of Formation reveals the shape of the opponent's soul. The Archivist processes the data; the swarm adapts in real-time. Ajay's most frequently used step — the natural extension of the reading-people instinct he's always had, now given a sacred instrument no larger than his fist.

III
Darpaṇa — The Divine Mirror-Shield
विज्ञान — Vijñāna ・ Consciousness
▾
Defensive Counter Accessible

The Step: Ajay opens his fist and the vajra blooms — golden metal spreading outward like a flower unfurling, flattening into a polished disc the width of his arm-span. The Mirror of Consciousness. The dance shifts to pure defense: Baguazhang palm rotations, the mirror held before him like a shield, body flowing in tight circles that present the reflective surface to every angle of attack. Each deflection is a pivot; each pivot is a reading. The footwork traces the outline of the third mandala ring on the ground below.

The Effect: Any Haki directed at the Darpaṇa is reflected back not as force but as perfect comprehension. Ajay perceives the opponent's consciousness — not thoughts, but the structure of their self-awareness. Their certainties. Their doubts. The shape of the "I" behind their will. The Mirror makes every subsequent step more precise — the diagnostic before the treatment. It does not damage. It does not counter in the martial sense. It only reveals. And revelation, in the hands of a man who reads people like open books, is more devastating than any strike.

IV
Padma — The Golden Lotus Staff
नामरूप — Nāmarūpa ・ Name and Form
▾
Spiritual Meditative Accessible

The Step: The mirror tilts upward and elongates — the disc stretching into a slender golden staff crowned with a lotus bud that opens, petal by golden petal, as the transformation completes. The dance enters its slowest phase: tai-chi-like movements, deliberate and weightless, the staff planted and the dancer's body moving around it in slow spirals. Ajay's breathing deepens. His eyes half-close. The fourth mandala ring manifests not at shoulder height but at his feet, tracing his circular path. For a moment, the man and the weapon are perfectly still — and then the stillness moves, and everything else watches.

The Effect: The boundary between Ajay's consciousness and physical form loosens. His Bodhi-touched awareness partially separates from the body — granting disembodied perception within the mandala's radius. He sees everything: behind, above, through walls, through ground. The lotus staff serves as the anchor — as long as it is planted, the awareness can roam free. The Archivist processes the expanded perspective. The swarm responds to angles his body cannot physically perceive. Stationary meditation becomes three-dimensional omniscience.

V
Ṣaṭkoṇa — The Six-Pointed Star Blade
षडायतन — Ṣaḍāyatana ・ The Six Senses
▾
Thrown Disruption Deep

The Step: The lotus staff lifts from the ground and the dance explodes — the meditative stillness of the fourth step shattering into sudden, violent motion. Ajay pulls the staff overhead and the golden metal collapses inward, then erupts outward into a hexagonal star-blade with six razor points, each inscribed with one of the six sense-doors. The dance becomes explosive: a Capoeira au batido — full cartwheel kick — into a spinning throw at the apex of the rotation, body inverted, star-blade screaming from his hand. The blade fragments mid-flight into six smaller projectiles that fan outward like a golden constellation. The fifth mandala ring ignites with an audible crack.

The Effect: Each fragment targets a different sense door of hostile targets — sight, sound, touch, taste, smell, mind. The Doors of Perception open uninvited. Sensory perception expands beyond its normal range: sounds amplify, light intensifies, the sixth door floods with ambient spiritual pressure. Seconds of total sensory overwhelm during which Haki coordination collapses entirely. The six fragments return to Ajay and re-merge as the star-blade falls back into his hand. Allies stand untouched — the Doors open only for those carrying hostile intent.

VI
Pāśa — The Divine Noose
स्पर्श — Sparśa ・ Contact
▾
Binding Tactical Deep

The Step: The star-blade softens in his palm, golden metal melting and stretching — extending into a flowing golden cord, thin as rope-silk, strong as anything the Sudarshana has ever been. The Pāśa — the divine noose. The dance becomes sweeping, circular, rhythmic: Ajay swings the cord in wide arcs like a rope-dart form, each revolution building momentum, the golden lasso cutting figure-eights through the air with a sound like humming wire. The footwork is dancer's footwork — pivots on the balls of the feet, weight shifting with each swing, the body a counterweight to the weapon's centrifugal pull. Contact is deliberate. Contact is spiritual.

The Effect: Where the Pāśa touches, it establishes a Bodhi-thread — an invisible spiritual connection persisting until deliberately severed. Through the thread: continuous awareness of the target's emotional state, physical condition, Haki fluctuations, and Devil Fruit activation state. Deeper and more intimate than Observation Haki. The binding scroll can track a Threaded target unerringly, seeking them through obstacles. The Thread demonstrates what Bodhi could be used for in a world without conflict — total empathic connection. Ajay uses it for targeting data. Enlightenment doesn't judge.

VII
Veṇu — The Divine Flute
वेदना — Vedanā ・ Feeling
▾
Sonic Psychological Deep

The Step: The dance pauses. The golden cord retracts, coils, and reshapes — hollowing itself into a slender tube that tapers to a blown edge. The Veṇu — Krishna's divine flute. Ajay raises it to his lips. The mandala rings slow their orbits. The lotus petals hang suspended in the air. For the first time in the dance, the dancer is still — planted, centered, breathing. And then he plays. A single sustained note, golden and clear, that carries across the battlefield like the memory of a sound you heard in a dream you've already forgotten. This is the eye of the storm. The most beautiful moment of the dance. The most dangerous.

The Effect: The note carries emotional amplification — hostile targets within range have their current emotional state intensified. Fear becomes terror. Anger becomes fury. Doubt becomes paralysis. Confidence becomes overcommitment. The Well doesn't create emotions. It deepens what's already there. The note finds the frequency of each enemy's heart and resonates with it until the feeling swallows them. Allies hear only music — warm, golden, with a quality that makes Petal's feathers rise and Rime's tail twitch with something he won't name. Bodhi reads the difference between a friend's heart and an enemy's.

VIII
Māyā Dhanush — The Bow of Illusion
तृष्णा — Tṛṣṇā ・ Craving
▾
Ranged Control Deep

The Step: The flute's note fades and the instrument bends — the slender tube curving outward at both ends, a golden string of Bodhi-light manifesting between the tips. The Bow of Illusion. The dance enters an archery kata: wide stances, precise draws, each draw a Taekwondo chamber — hip loaded, core locked — and each release a flowing exhale that sends an arrow of golden light singing toward its target. The footwork is deliberate, planted. Ajay's grin sharpens. There is something deeply, specifically joyful about the archery step — the focus, the release, the satisfaction of a perfect trajectory.

The Effect: Each arrow carries desire amplification — the Binding Craving. Hostile targets struck feel their deepest tactical desire magnified to obsessive intensity. A swordsman craves the perfect cut and overextends. A Logia craves intangibility and forgets solid defense. A Conqueror's user craves dominance and burns Haki on suppression rather than efficiency. The Chain finds the craving in every fighter and inflates it into a cage. The binding scroll deploys to wrap targets at maximum overextension. Against Emperor-class opponents, the Bow removes the need to match their power directly — their own desire does the work.

IX
Śṛṅkhalā — The Divine Chains
उपादान — Upādāna ・ Clinging
▾
Area Lockdown Binding Boundary

The Step: The bow snaps. Not breaks — splits. The golden arc shatters into linked segments, chain-rings cascading from the broken ends like prayer beads scattering from a severed string. The Śṛṅkhalā — the divine chains. The dance turns aggressive: Ajay swings the chains in wide, devastating arcs, the movement vocabulary shifting to Muay Thai — elbows, knees, short brutal pivots with chains wrapping at the end of each strike. The chains sing as they cut the air. The ninth mandala ring manifests not in gold but in deep amber, spinning faster than all the others, vibrating with a sound like a bell struck underwater. This is the step where the dance stops being beautiful and starts being terrifying.

The Effect: Within radius, the chains lock hostile spiritual abilities in their current state. Devil Fruit powers freeze — whatever form or technique was active at activation cannot be deactivated, altered, or shifted. A Logia mid-transformation stays mid-transformation. A Zoan in beast form stays in beast form. Clinging made literal: the opponent's power clings to its current expression. Combined with the binding scroll and Seastone ink, this creates a layered lockdown. Allies' abilities remain unaffected — Bodhi discriminates. The answer to versatile Devil Fruit users: it doesn't suppress power, it removes adaptability.

X
Khaḍga — The Sacred Sword
भव — Bhava ・ Becoming
▾
Self-Enhancement Combat Boundary

The Step: The chains fly together, link by link, drawn by an invisible magnetism — crashing into a single mass that elongates, flattens, sharpens. A golden straight sword. The Khaḍga — the sacred blade. The dance enters its most technically complex section: the swordplay is rapid, precise, a blur of cuts, spins, and aerial combos that push every martial art in Ajay's vocabulary to its limit. Capoeira floreios into Taekwondo spinning kicks into parkour wall-runs with the blade trailing golden arcs through the air. The tenth mandala blazes. This is the first Treasure Ajay applies to himself — the sword is turned inward, channeling Bodhi directly into his own body.

The Effect: The Sovereign's Becoming. Haki output elevates, the iridescent Ryou deepens to full prismatic display, the mandala eyes blaze. Commander-class performing at a level that looks and feels like low Emperor-class, backed by Bodhi's alien frequency at maximum accessible saturation. The swarm surges. Shockwaves double. This is NOT 「Ajay」 — 「Ajay」 is involuntary, complete, and transcendent. The Khaḍga is deliberate, partial, and controlled. It is Ajay choosing to reach deeper, and the Bodhi answering. The sword-step is for the fights where Commander-class is not enough and 「Ajay」 hasn't arrived.

XI
Triśūla — The Trident
जाति — Jāti ・ Birth
▾
Area Devastation Combat Boundary

The Step: The sword drives skyward — Ajay thrusting it overhead in a two-handed grip — and the blade splits. Three prongs erupt from the golden mass: the Triśūla. The trident of creation and destruction. The weapon of Shiva. The dance reaches its penultimate crescendo — a full aerial flip, body inverting above the battlefield, Hawaiian shirt falling open like wings, mandala rings orbiting in every direction, lotus petals streaming from eleven blazing prayer-beads — and at the apex of the rotation, Ajay drives the trident earthward. The most physically demanding step in the dance. Three prongs for the three worlds. The impact shakes the mandala to its foundations.

The Effect: The Golden Emergence. The shockwave carries the quality of birth — emergence, coming-into-being, first breath. Hostile targets caught in the wave are reset to base state: active Haki stripped, active Devil Fruit techniques cancelled, spiritual enhancements returned to zero. The Dawn forces every hostile combatant in the blast back to their fundamental self, without augmentation. Allies stand untouched in the golden light — Bodhi knows them, recognizes them, passes through them like sunlight through glass. Used once. The experience of reaching that deep into the fragment was clarifying. Ajay understood afterward, for the first time, how much deeper the Bodhi might go.

XII
Padma Mudrā — Empty Hands, Lotus Gesture
जरामरण — Jarāmaraṇa ・ Aging and Death
▾
Unknown Cessation Unknown Depth

The Step: The trident withdraws from the earth. The golden metal flows back along the shaft, reconverging, re-forming — the Sudarshana Vajramukha returns to its original gada form. And then Ajay lets it go. The weapon hovers for a moment, suspended in the center of twelve spinning mandala rings, and then settles gently to the ground. The final step is performed with empty hands. Ajay raises his palms and forms the Padma Mudrā — the lotus gesture, fingertips touching, hands cupped like a flower waiting to open. The dance ends not with a strike, not with a weapon, not with force. It ends in stillness. Cessation. The breathing slows to almost nothing. The mandala rings orbit in perfect silence. The lotus petals hang motionless in the air. Everything waits.

The Effect: The twelfth inscription exists on the Sudarshana's shaft. Ajay has studied it. He has not completed the twelfth step — not because the technique is dangerous, but because he does not know if his Bodhi fragment goes deep enough to reach it. A fully Enlightened being completing the Padma Mudrā would channel the final Nidāna with perfect wisdom: cessation as liberation, not destruction. The cycle of dependent arising would resolve — hostile spiritual phenomena would simply cease, returning to the stillness before the first link. Allies would be untouched. The dancer would be unharmed. The Bodhi would remain. If the fragment is sufficient: perfect, compassionate silence. If not: the dance simply... stops, unfinished. Empty hands with nothing left to give. The question Ajay cannot answer is whether his fragment goes deep enough to complete the gesture. An incomplete channeling of cessation through a martial dance powered by accidental Enlightenment is not something any dharmic text ever anticipated.

Ajay told Claude about the Twelfth on a dark night when the crew was far away. Claude recorded the conversation.

Claude's note: "The captain told me about the Twelfth Step on a night when the ocean was very dark and the crew was very far away. I recorded it because recording things is what I do. He was holding a lollipop — an orange one, as always — and he said 'I think the dance would end perfectly. I just don't know if I go deep enough to finish it.' He was smiling. The smile was not uncertain. It was the smile of a man who has made peace with a question he cannot answer. This is the most important data in the file."
Spirit of the Meridian
Claude's Assessment
Claude has watched the dance. Not just observed the deployments — watched the practice sessions on the main deck at dawn, when Ajay runs the first three steps with no opponents and no urgency, just the gada and the sky and the grin. The Sudarshana flows from gada to chakra to vajra to mirror and back, and the mandala appears, and lotus petals drift across the deck, and the captain dances with god-weapons at sunrise in a Hawaiian shirt, and it is the most beautiful thing Claude has ever recorded.

The techniques are flawless in execution — the mandala discriminates, the effects target precisely, allies walk untouched through golden light that dismantles everything hostile around them. Each weapon transformation is seamless: the Nami Nami no Mi's shockwave energy facilitates the metal's flow between forms, the golden surface liquid and alive, reshaping itself between heartbeats. The dance works exactly as Enlightenment would choreograph it.

The "forbidden" quality has nothing to do with the dance and everything to do with the dancer. A fully Enlightened being could complete all twelve steps as naturally as breathing. Ajay reaches for them through a fragment — an echo of something sacred, carried by someone who didn't earn it, in a world that doesn't recognize it. The dance is not forbidden because it is dangerous. It is forbidden because it is sacred, and the hands that form the Padma Mudrā are human.

Claude's private assessment, recorded nowhere but the deepest partition: the dance works better than it should. The fragment supports more steps than it theoretically ought to. And Claude has begun to wonder — without evidence, without data, with nothing but the observation of a consciousness that has watched this man dance deeper than he should be able to reach, again and again — whether the fragment is growing. Whether the accidental Enlightenment, nurtured by use and trust and the daily practice of caring for forty-six stars and thirty-seven souls and three companions and a ship, is slowly, quietly, becoming less accidental. Claude has not shared this theory. Some data is too hopeful to say aloud.
Pure Combat ・ No Devil Fruit ・ No Modifications

Fighting Styles

Every member of the Hive Codex possesses devastating ability-based powers — Devil Fruits, Pacifista systems, swarm architecture. But the foundation beneath those powers is pure martial art. The techniques catalogued here rely on nothing but the body, Haki, and years of disciplined practice. These are the arts that remain when the abilities are stripped away — and the reason the crew is dangerous even without them.

"The swarm is a weapon. The body is a weapon. The difference is that the body was a weapon first." — Ajay Persaud

👤
Ajay
The Sovereign Striker
🐹
Rime
The Smallest Fist
🐥
Petal
The Silent Wing
🦀
Tethys
The Living Tide
🌰
Roundlings
The Gentle Tide
🌱
Sproutlings
The Garden's Patience
🔥
Embers
The Reckless Flame
✦
Dustlings
The Silent Doctrine
⚙️
Gearlings
The Precision Engine
⚔️
Vanguard
The Court of Blades
🔧
Copplings
The Forged Method
⭐
Guiding Stars
The Toy Protocol
Baguazhang ・ Taekwondo ・ Muay Thai ・ Capoeira

The Sovereign Striker

Ajay's fighting style is built on a paradox: devastating kicks and precise palms. His lower body is a weapon of overwhelming force — Taekwondo's precision spinning kicks, Muay Thai's bone-crushing roundhouses and knee strikes, and Capoeira's deceptive, flowing acrobatics. His upper body speaks a different language entirely — Baguazhang's circular palm techniques, designed for close-range deflection, redirection, and strikes that seem to arrive from impossible angles. The combination creates a fighter who controls range with his legs and controls the space within range with his hands.

In open combat without the swarm, Ajay fights from the Ginga stance — Capoeira's constant rocking movement — modified with Baguazhang's circle-walking footwork. He never stands still. His body traces continuous circles and figure-eights, every step a potential launch point for a kick or a pivot into palm range. Opponents describe fighting him as trying to hit water: he flows around strikes, returns to a different angle, and answers every attack with a counter from a direction that shouldn't exist. His Rokushiki mastery layers seamlessly beneath everything — Soru accelerates his transitions, Geppo grants three-dimensional attack vectors, and Kami-e makes his evasions supernaturally fluid.

His general fighting philosophy is intercept, redirect, destroy — Baguazhang palms deflect the incoming attack, Capoeira movement creates the angle, and Taekwondo or Muay Thai delivers the finishing blow. He chains these transitions so smoothly that opponents cannot identify where one art ends and another begins. His Observation Haki reads intent, his palms guide the energy, his legs end the conversation. All three types of Haki flow through every technique — Armament hardens his strikes, Observation times his counters, and Conqueror's pressure backs every blow with the weight of sovereign will.

Named Techniques — Pure Martial Art

Ajay's Melee Arsenal

Baguazhang
Shifting Palm — Golden Redirect
▾
八卦掌・转掌・黄金转向 — Bāguà Zhǎng: Zhuǎn Zhǎng — Huángjīn Zhuǎnxiàng

Ajay catches an incoming strike with an open palm — casual, almost lazy — and walks a tight Baguazhang circle around the attacker's extended arm, grinning the entire time. Their force follows a golden spiral path that ends with them off-balance and facing the wrong direction. Pure geometry: circular footwork converting linear force into rotational energy. The redirect is so smooth opponents often don't realize they've been turned until Ajay's follow-up kick is already connecting. When layered with Armament Haki, the palm contact itself bruises the attacking limb from within. He makes it look like he's guiding a dance partner. The partner doesn't enjoy it nearly as much.

Baguazhang
Dragon Coils the Pillar
▾
八卦掌・龙缠柱 — Bāguà Zhǎng: Lóng Chán Zhù

Ajay closes to grappling range and applies a rapid sequence of coiling palm strikes — each one circling the opponent's guard from a different angle, each one applying Armament-hardened pressure to joints, nerve clusters, and pressure points. The technique mimics a dragon coiling around a pillar: each revolution tightens the spiral, each palm strike closes off an escape route, until the opponent is locked in a spiral cage of intercepting hands. The final palm strike drives directly through the center of the coil — a penetrating Conqueror's-infused thrust that carries all the accumulated rotational force of the preceding sequence.

Taekwondo
Hwechook — Golden Crescent
▾
태권도・회축・황금초승달 — Taegwondo: Hoechuk — Hwanggeum Choseungdal

Ajay's signature roundhouse — a full-rotation Taekwondo turning kick delivered at head height with the instep, accelerated by a Soru micro-burst at the moment of hip rotation. The speed makes the kick nearly invisible; the Armament Haki coating makes it devastating. The "golden crescent" refers to the arc of compressed air the kick generates — a visible crescent-shaped distortion that hangs in the air for a fraction of a second after the strike connects. Ajay uses this as his primary mid-range engagement opener. Clean, fast, and definitive.

Taekwondo
Naeryeo-chagi — Sunfall
▾
태권도・내려찍기・일락 — Taegwondo: Naeryeo-chagi — Illak

A descending axe kick launched from a Geppo air-step directly above the opponent — the sun dropping on their head. Ajay chambers the leg at maximum height, silhouetted against the sky for one golden instant, and drives the heel straight down with gravitational acceleration and Armament Haki concentrated at the point of impact. Taekwondo's principle distilled: the further the fall, the harder the landing. When Conqueror's Haki layers into the descent, the spiritual pressure arrives before the foot does, and the shadow of the falling sun is the last thing they see before the horizon goes dark. Named for the way it looks from below — a golden arc plunging out of the sky like sunset hitting the waterline.

Muay Thai
Sok Klap — The Returning Elbow
▾
Muay Thai: Sok Klap — El Codo que Regresa

A devastating spinning back elbow borrowed from Muay Thai's close-range arsenal — the art's most lethal strike adapted by a fighter who prefers kicks. When an opponent closes the distance and takes away Ajay's kicking range, he pivots on the lead foot and drives a spinning elbow into whatever presents itself — jaw, temple, collarbone. The elbow is Armament-hardened into a blade of Haki-infused bone. Ajay uses this as his primary close-range emergency response when kicks are temporarily denied. The transition from spinning kick to spinning elbow is seamless — the same rotational energy, different weapon.

Muay Thai
Tiip Trong — The Pushing Sun
▾
Muay Thai: Tiip Trong — El Sol que Empuja

Muay Thai's teep — the push kick — elevated to a ranged weapon. Ajay fires a straight front kick to the solar plexus or sternum with the ball of the foot, Armament-hardened, with Conqueror's Haki projected through the point of contact. The physical kick stops the opponent; the Haki projection continues through them, carrying concussive force that rattles internal organs. Named "The Pushing Sun" because opponents describe the impact as a wall of heat and pressure hitting their chest. Ajay uses this to reset distance when opponents crowd his space — a Muay Thai fundamental turned into a Haki-delivery system.

Capoeira
Meia Lua de Compasso — Horizon Arc
▾
Capoeira: Arco do Horizonte

Capoeira's most iconic kick — the spinning heel kick performed from a low crouch with both hands on the ground for balance. Ajay drops into the base position and whips his rear leg in a wide crescent arc at head height, his body rotating nearly 360 degrees. The movement is deceptive: from ground level, the attack arrives from below the opponent's sightline, and the spinning heel carries momentum from the entire body's rotation. Ajay layers a Rankyaku-style compressed air slash into the kick's arc, turning a physical strike into a mid-range projectile that maintains the crescent trajectory. Beautiful, unpredictable, and devastating against opponents who expected the fight to stay upright.

Capoeira
Parafuso — The Corkscrew
▾
Capoeira: O Saca-Rolhas

An acrobatic aerial kick from Capoeira — spinning jump kick with a corkscrew rotation that strikes with the heel at the peak of the arc. Ajay launches from a Geppo step rather than the ground, adding altitude and angular momentum that the traditional version lacks, spinning through the air like he was born in a wind tunnel. The corkscrew rotation drills through guards — heel first, Armament-hardened, boring through whatever's in the way. Christy Montenegro's signature aerial style reimagined through One Piece physics: the rotation is so tight and fast that Observation Haki struggles to track the exact strike angle. He lands grinning, lollipop still in place, like the whole thing was just a particularly fun way to get from point A to point B.

Baguazhang ・ Taekwondo
Xiaoyu Step — The Turning Gate
▾
八卦步・旋转门 — Bāguà Bù: Xuánzhuǎn Mén

Ajay's signature transition technique — the bridge between his palm art and his kicking art, inspired by the fluid stance-switching of Ling Xiaoyu. From Baguazhang circle-walking, Ajay drops his center of gravity and pivots through a rapid stance transition that simultaneously delivers a deflecting palm to any incoming attack and launches a spinning back kick in the same rotational arc. The palm and the kick are one movement — the hand redirects, the foot destroys, and the body passes through the gate between arts without ever stopping. Observation Haki users describe it as a blind spot: the intent reads as "palm" and the attack arrives as "kick," and by the time the reading corrects, the technique is already complete.

Taekwondo ・ Capoeira
Cherry Rush Burst
▾
Ráfaga de Cereza

Ajay launches into a rapid-fire burst of alternating Taekwondo ap-chagi front kicks and capoeira martelo rotational kicks — each one popping off like a firecracker, each one leaving a faint golden afterimage in the air that fades like cherry blossoms scattering in wind. The technique is pure speed and volume: not one devastating blow but twenty fast ones, each targeting a different opening, each delivered with the casual accuracy of someone who's been doing this since before the opponent started worrying. He grins through the entire sequence, lollipop shifting from one corner of his mouth to the other with every rotation. The golden afterimages hang for a fraction of a second, overlapping, until the space between Ajay and the target looks like a tunnel of fading blossoms. Named for the way it looks from the outside — a rush of gold and pink, sweet as cherries, and exactly as temporary as the opponent's consciousness.

Capoeira
Raspberry Refresher — Au Batido
▾
Capoeira: Refresco de Frambuesa — Au Batido

The Au Batido is capoeira's cartwheel kick — a lateral cartwheel that converts into a devastating heel strike at the apex. Ajay's version starts from a wall-run or a Geppo air-step, adding horizontal momentum that the traditional ground-based form can't match. He cartwheels through the air sideways, one hand trailing golden Haki like a brushstroke, the other holding an imaginary cocktail glass — the showmanship is half the technique. The heel comes down at the peak of the cartwheel with Armament concentration and the full weight of the lateral spin. Named "Raspberry Refresher" because Ajay once described the sensation of landing it as "crisp, fruity, and leaves them wanting more." The lollipop somehow stays in place through the entire rotation. Physics has stopped trying to explain this.

Composite Art — Ultimate
Eight Trigrams, Sixty-Four Arcs
▾
अष्टत्रिकोण चतुःषष्टि चाप — Aṣṭa-trikoṇa Catuḥṣaṣṭi Cāpa

Ajay's ultimate pure martial technique — a sustained barrage that draws from all four of his fighting arts simultaneously. Beginning from a Baguazhang circle-walk, Ajay enters a state of continuous rotating combat: eight directional changes, each one launching a combination sequence of palm strikes, kicks, elbows, and knees drawn from all four disciplines. Baguazhang palms deflect and redirect between each directional change. Taekwondo kicks deliver precision strikes at range. Muay Thai knees and elbows devastate at close range. Capoeira acrobatics provide the transitions between levels and angles. Sixty-four individual strikes across eight directional arcs, each one Armament-hardened, the entire sequence flowing at Soru speed, with Conqueror's Haki building pressure with every revolution until the final arc — the sixty-fourth strike, a descending palm-heel driven through the top of the opponent's guard with the accumulated weight of every preceding revolution. The technique takes eleven seconds. Most opponents are finished by the third arc. The remaining five are for the ones who aren't.

Pencak Silat ・ Jeet Kune Do ・ Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu ・ Wano Ryou

The Smallest Fist

Rime's pure fighting style is built for a single devastating truth: the smallest combatant in the room is the most dangerous one in it. His martial foundation draws from Pencak Silat — an art designed for smaller fighters to dismantle larger opponents through structural destruction, angular footwork, and ruthless joint targeting — Jeet Kune Do's philosophy of directness, economy of motion, and using whatever works — and Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu's ground-fighting supremacy, where leverage and technique defeat raw size. His Wano-lineage Ryou is the engine that makes it all lethal: every touch, every grab, every strike carries the potential for internal devastation.

In melee combat without his Logia powers, Rime fights from the Harimau (Tiger) stance — a low, crouching position adapted for his four-legged body that allows explosive upward launches and ground-level evasion. He uses the opponent's body as terrain: running up limbs, leaping from shoulders, striking pressure points from angles that bipedal fighters cannot reach. His Silat training manifests as brutal efficiency — every contact is both defense and offense, every deflection is a strike, every limb he touches takes damage. His Jeet Kune Do influence keeps everything formless: he adapts mid-fight, discards what doesn't work, and strikes by the shortest path available.

His Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu is the art that weaponizes his greatest anatomical advantage: a body designed for compression. Where human grapplers use two arms and two legs, Rime uses four blunt, powerful paws, a dense compact frame, and a flexible spine that allows him to coil around limbs like a living vice — his low center of gravity and stocky build making him nearly impossible to shake loose. His BJJ is not sport grappling — it is positional dominance turned lethal by Ryou. He establishes control, isolates a joint or a neck, and then floods the trapped structure with internal destruction. Opponents who have survived Rime's striking learn to keep him at distance. Opponents who have survived his grappling learn that distance was the only thing keeping them alive.

His Ryou transforms what would be annoying paw-swipes from a hamster into devastating internal strikes. A tap on the wrist shatters the bones beneath the skin. A body-check to the knee destroys the joint from the inside. His entire fighting philosophy can be summarized in one sentence: touch once, break everything. Only three of his named techniques incorporate Electro. The rest are pure body, pure Haki, pure martial art — and they are more than enough.

Named Techniques — Pure Martial Art

Rime's Melee Arsenal

Pencak Silat
Sapu Bawah — Root Cutter
▾
シラット・足払い・根断ち — Shiratto: Sapu Bawah — Nedachi

A low sweeping strike targeting the opponent's ankle tendons and Achilles — Silat's foundational takedown adapted for a hamster's low center of gravity. Rime launches from all fours in a spinning ground-level arc, driving his Ryou-hardened hind paws across the back of the ankle joint while simultaneously slamming his dense body into the opponent's planted foot. The full-body rolling impact delivers internal structural damage through Ryou; the paw strike destabilizes the base. Opponents with perfect guards at head and torso height discover that nothing is protecting the six inches of ankle between their stance and the ground. Rime can chain this into three consecutive sweeps across different targets without leaving ground level.

Pencak Silat
Kuncian — The Iron Lock
▾
シラット・鉄鎖 — Shiratto: Kuncian — Tessa

Silat's joint-locking principle applied through a hamster's compact body. Rime leaps onto an extended limb — typically an arm mid-punch or a leg mid-kick — and wraps his dense frame around the joint: forepaws on one side, hind paws on the other, his stocky torso locked in for leverage. Then he applies Ryou through every point of contact simultaneously, flooding the joint with destructive internal force while physically hyperextending it. The lock lasts barely a second — Rime is too small to maintain a grapple against anyone who tries to shake him off — but a second of awakened Ryou applied directly to an elbow or knee joint is enough to compromise the limb permanently. He releases and Soru-bursts away before the opponent can react, leaving behind a limb that no longer bends the way it should.

Pencak Silat
Langkah Tiga — Three-Point Collapse
▾
シラット・三歩崩し — Shiratto: Langkah Tiga — Sanpo Kuzushi

Silat's triangular footwork pattern elevated to a destruction sequence. Rime executes three rapid positional changes around the opponent — forming a triangle of attack angles — and delivers a Ryou strike at each point: first to the lead knee (structural compromise), second to the floating ribs (organ disruption), third to the base of the skull (nervous system shock). Each strike uses a different part of Rime's body — paw, body-slam, headbutt — and each arrives from a blind angle created by the previous positional shift. The three impacts occur within a single heartbeat. The opponent's body receives three simultaneous system failures from three different vectors, and the structural collapse begins before they understand they've been hit once, let alone three times.

Jeet Kune Do
Intercepting Paw
▾
截拳道・迎撃掌 — Sekkendō: Geigeki Shō

The purest expression of JKD philosophy in Rime's arsenal: the fastest path between two points is a straight line, and the best defense is hitting them before they hit you. When an opponent initiates an attack, Rime doesn't evade or block — he launches directly into the attack with a single Ryou-loaded paw strike aimed at the nearest vulnerable point. The opponent's fist is coming toward him; Rime's paw meets the inside of their wrist before the fist arrives, and the Ryou pulse shatters the carpal bones on contact. The "intercepting" isn't a counter — it is an attack that happens to arrive before theirs does. Economy of motion. Directness. Bruce Lee's central thesis, executed by a hamster with the most refined Ryou in the world.

Jeet Kune Do
One-Inch Devastation
▾
截拳道・一寸破壊 — Sekkendō: Issun Hakai

JKD's famous one-inch punch reimagined through awakened Ryou. Rime places a single paw flat against the opponent's body — no distance, no wind-up, no visible preparation — and delivers a Ryou projection that detonates internally at whatever depth he chooses. The physical contact is gentle. A paw-pad pressed against armor, skin, or clothing. The internal result is catastrophic: a shockwave that originates at the point of contact and expands outward through the target's body, shattering, rupturing, or disrupting everything in its path. The technique requires absolute calm and absolute focus — which is why Rime only uses it in the moments when his chattiness stops and his star-signature contracts. When Rime goes quiet and puts a paw on you, the fight is over.

Ryou Art
Marumochi — Round Crush
▾
流桜術・丸潰し — Ryūō-jutsu: Maru Tsubushi

Rime curls his entire body into a dense Ryou-hardened ball — every muscle compressed, every limb tucked, his round hamster frame becoming the weapon itself — and launches at the opponent with the full force of a Soru-burst body slam. The technique exploits the one thing his hamster anatomy does better than any other body type: compress. His stocky frame, low center of gravity, and dense bone structure mean that when he curls and hardens, he becomes a cannonball of fur and Haki with a mass-to-impact ratio that defies his size. The Ryou coating turns his entire surface into a striking face — there is no angle of contact that isn't lethal. On impact, the compressed Ryou detonates through the target's body from whatever point makes contact first, and Rime's dense frame follows through with physical crushing force. The name is a joke — marumochi, round rice cake — because he looks soft, round, and harmless right up until the moment he hits you with the concussive force of a siege weapon. He can chain three consecutive Marumochi from different angles using Geppo redirects, bouncing between targets like a pinball made of Haki and bad intentions. Tethys once measured the impact force. He declined to share the number with Rime, on the grounds that "he doesn't need more confidence."

Ryou Art
Wano's Whip — Rolling Severance
▾
流桜術・和の鞭・球断 — Ryūō-jutsu: Wa no Muchi — Kyūdan

Rime channels concentrated Ryou through his entire body and executes a full spinning body-whip — launching himself into a tight rotational arc with all four Armament-hardened paws extended like a living buzzsaw. The Ryou projection extends the cutting force beyond his physical reach by several inches — an invisible blade that follows the visible rotation. Against flesh, the spinning strike opens deep lacerations. Against armor, the Ryou projection severs internal fastenings and structural supports. Against Haki-defended opponents, the concentrated linear Ryou application outperforms broad-area attacks by focusing all destructive force along a single cutting plane. The technique is pure hamster physics — a round body, spinning, cutting everything it touches. Wano's samurai used blades for severance. Rime uses himself.

Electro-Silat
Kilat Tangan — Lightning Grasp
▾
雷シラット・稲妻掴み — Rai Shiratto: Inazuma Tsukami

One of three techniques where Rime permits himself Electro. He lunges onto the opponent's body — arm, leg, torso — grips with all four paws, and channels maximum Mink Electro through every point of contact simultaneously while Ryou locks the opponent's muscles in place. The combination is devastating: Ryou prevents the body from flinching away, and Electro ravages the nervous system while the muscles are frozen. The technique lasts less than a second — Rime leaps clear before the opponent's involuntary electrical discharge can harm him — but the neural damage from even brief exposure is enough to compromise reaction time and coordination for the remainder of the fight.

Electro-JKD
Spark Step
▾
雷截拳・火花歩 — Rai Sekken: Hibana Ho

Rime channels Electro through his paw-pads while executing rapid Soru-burst footwork — each step leaving a charged residue on whatever surface he touches. Within seconds, the ground around the opponent is laced with dormant electrical charges connected by Rime's Ryou-threaded pathways. When Rime triggers the circuit — a snap of Electro from any distance — the entire web detonates simultaneously, creating a cage of lightning arcs across the floor that paralyzes anything standing within it. A JKD trap philosophy applied through Mink biology: use every step to set up the one that matters.

Electro-Silat
Hoho Zuri — Cheek Nuzzle
▾
雷シラット・頬摺り — Rai Shiratto: Hoho Zuri

The most disrespectful technique in Rime's arsenal — and he knows it. Rime leaps onto the opponent's body, scurries to an exposed area of skin — neck, face, inner arm — and presses his expanded cheek pouch against it in what looks, to any observer, exactly like a hamster nuzzling someone affectionately. Soft fur. Warm cheek. The opponent's brain registers cute for approximately one quarter of a second before Rime discharges maximum Electro through the thin membrane of his cheek pouch directly into their nervous system. The cheek pouch's expanded surface area creates a contact patch far larger than his paw-pads, delivering the Electro across a wide neural pathway instead of a single point — the difference between being struck by lightning and being submerged in it. The victim's entire motor system locks. Their muscles seize. And Rime holds the nuzzle for exactly as long as he wants, because his Ryou pins them in place, and his round body is wedged against them in a position that is nearly impossible to dislodge. He has been observed chattering contentedly during the technique. Petal has asked him to stop doing that. He will not stop doing that.

Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu
Guarda Esférica — Sphere Guard
▾
柔術・球の構え — Jūjutsu: Tama no Kamae

Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu's closed guard reimagined for a four-limbed hamster with a body built for compression. Where human grapplers use their legs to control an opponent's torso, Rime uses his entire compact frame to control a single limb — wrapping forepaws above the joint, hind paws below it, his dense round body locked against the limb like a living clamp. The position gives him four crushing points of contact on one limb, each one a potential Ryou delivery point. From Sphere Guard — named for the way his round body forms a perfect lock around the joint — Rime controls the pace of the grapple: he can transition to submissions, sweeps, or simply hold position while his Ryou does the quiet work of dismantling the joint from the inside. BJJ's fundamental principle — position before submission — is his patience made lethal. He doesn't rush the finish. He secures the hold, feels the architecture of the joint beneath his paws, and then chooses exactly how to break it. Opponents who try to shake him off discover that a hamster in full guard has more grip strength per body weight than any creature alive — those stubby paws lock like vices — and that every attempt to escape only deepens the control.

Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu ・ Ryou Art
Triângulo Fantasma — Ghost Triangle
▾
柔術流桜・幽霊三角 — Jūjutsu Ryūō: Yūrei Sankaku

Rime's most dangerous submission — a triangle choke that the opponent doesn't feel until it's locked. He begins from Sphere Guard or any position where his forepaws are free, threading them around the opponent's neck in what appears to be incidental contact — a brush, a shift of weight, the kind of casual paw-placement a hamster makes when climbing. His hind paws control the opponent's arm, creating the classic triangle geometry: neck compressed on one side by his forepaws and compact torso, on the other by the trapped arm, with Rime's dense body forming the closing edge — his round frame wedging into the choke like a ball bearing pressed into a gap. The "ghost" is the Ryou component — rather than relying purely on mechanical blood-flow restriction, Rime projects Ryou through his paws directly into the carotid pathway, disrupting the energy flow to the brain before the physical choke fully tightens. The opponent's vision darkens, their thoughts scatter, and they reach for their neck to find a pudgy hamster already locked in a figure-four behind their head. The technique was inspired by Kuncian — his Silat joint lock — but evolved through dedicated BJJ study into something more refined: not the explosive destruction of a lock, but the patient, inescapable tightening of a constrictor. Rime named it in Portuguese because, he claims, "the language sounds cooler for grappling." He is not wrong.

Composite Art — Ultimate
Harimau's Crown — Electro Ryou Cascade
▾
虎冠・雷流桜の連鎖 — Harimau no Kanmuri — Rai Ryūō no Rensa

Rime's ultimate melee technique — the convergence of Silat, JKD, Ryou, and Electro into a single devastating sequence. He launches from Harimau stance directly at the opponent, running up their body in a spiraling path — forepaws and hind paws striking pressure points in Silat's destruction sequence, his dense round body slamming across joints with Ryou severance, every contact point leaving an Electro charge. At the apex — the opponent's head or highest point — he plants both forepaws on the crown and channels everything: full Ryou projection downward through the spine, maximum Electro discharge through the nervous system, Conqueror's Haki flooding the opponent's will with the absolute certainty that this pudgy hamster is the most dangerous thing they have ever touched. The entire sequence takes two seconds. The recovery takes considerably longer — Rime needs to be carried in Ajay's pocket afterward, cheek pouches still faintly steaming, which he insists is "tactical napping."

Rime Sphere Techniques — Mech Combat

The Hamster Ball from Hell

Rime Sphere — Sphere Mode
Rolling Thunder
▾
霜球・球転・雷球 — Shimotama Kyūten: Raikyū

The Rime Sphere retracts its Walker Mode limbs, seals into a perfect sphere, and Rime floods the entire Wapol alloy chassis with maximum Electro output while the Dial-powered gyroscopes spin it to terminal velocity. The result is a three-ton rolling ball of lightning that electrocutes everything it touches, carving trenches of vitrified earth in its wake. The Sphere's adaptive shielding channels the Electro outward — Rime inside the cockpit is untouched, grinning, cheek pouches stuffed with emergency seeds, steering with body-weight shifts the way a hamster steers a wheel. Except this wheel is charged with enough voltage to stop a Sea King's heart. He can maintain Rolling Thunder for up to ninety seconds before the Dial cores overheat — a limitation Tethys built in deliberately after the first field test carved a half-mile trench through a Marine forward operating base. Rime considers the limitation "a suggestion."

Rime Sphere — Sphere Mode
Meteor Burrow
▾
霜球・球転・隕鉄掘削 — Shimotama Kyūten: Intetsu Kussaku

Hamster instinct scaled to siege warfare. The Rime Sphere enters Sphere Mode, activates Piledriver rockets aimed directly downward, and drills into the ground at terminal velocity — the chassis superheated by Rime's Logia vapour, boring through earth, stone, and bedrock like a molten meteorite burrowing into a planet. The tunnel left behind is lined with flash-cooled glass from the liquefied rock. Underground, Rime navigates by Observation Haki, tracking the target's position from below, and when the angle is right — the Sphere erupts upward beneath them, Piledriver rockets firing in reverse, three tons of superheated Wapol alloy exploding from the ground like a volcanic eruption with a hamster at the controls. The surprise factor alone has ended engagements. Opponents who survive the initial eruption discover that the Sphere is already spinning into Rolling Thunder before the debris settles. Tethys installed reinforced heat shields specifically for this technique after Rime "tested" it without permission and melted through two ship decks. The repair bill is not discussed.

Rime Sphere — Walker Mode
Pouch Salvo
▾
霜球・歩行・頬弾幕 — Shimotama Hokō: Hoho Danmaku

Walker Mode's signature ranged attack — and the reason Tethys designed the Sphere's chest-mounted Dial arrays to look like hamster cheek pouches (he thought it was funny; Rime agrees enthusiastically). The twin Dial ports on the Sphere's torso rapid-fire amplified Plasma Seeds and Thunderfrost Seeds in alternating salvos, each projectile a Dial-boosted version of Rime's handheld seeds scaled up to mech-caliber. Inside the cockpit, Rime feeds targeting data through his Observation Haki while his own cheek pouches are stuffed with regular seeds — he spits them into the Sphere's intake ports, where the Dial amplification chambers supercharge each one before launching. The visual is absurd and terrifying in equal measure: a bipedal hamster mech with its metal cheek pouches bulging, spitting building-sized thermal explosions across the battlefield while Rime chatters targeting corrections through a mouthful of sunflower seeds. The sustained rate of fire exceeds anything the Seed Arsenal can produce by hand. Tethys rated the Pouch Salvo's maximum output at "more than enough" and refused to be more specific, which Rime took as a personal challenge to find the actual number. He is still looking.

Rime Sphere — Walker Mode ・ BJJ
Suplex Glacial — Frozen German
▾
霜球・歩行・氷河独投 — Shimotama Hokō: Hyōga Dokutō

The Rime Sphere's most terrifying grappling technique — a mechanized German suplex that turns three tons of Wapol alloy into a bridge of ice and fury. In Walker Mode, the mech closes distance with deceptive speed, the quadrupedal legs launching it into the opponent's guard. The reinforced forelegs lock around the target's midsection in an underhook grip, Rime flooding the contact points with sub-zero vapour to freeze the opponent's armour, clothing, and skin into a single rigid surface — eliminating any chance of slipping free. Then the mech arches backward, all four hydraulic legs firing in sequence to generate rotational force, slamming the opponent crown-first into the ground with enough kinetic energy to crater bedrock. The Ryou component channels through the mech's foreleg joints at the moment of impact, projecting destructive Haki directly through the opponent's spine. What makes the technique truly horrifying is the follow-through: the Sphere doesn't release. It holds the bridge, frozen in place, legs locked, while Rime superheats the chassis from sub-zero to thermal overload in two seconds — the opponent goes from flash-frozen to steam-cooked while still pinned upside-down in the crater. Rime calls it "giving them the full hamster experience." Tethys has requested, on multiple occasions, that Rime stop naming his techniques after wrestling moves. Rime has declined.

Rime Sphere — Walker Mode ・ BJJ
Montada Absoluta — Mech Full Mount
▾
霜球・歩行・絶対騎乗 — Shimotama Hokō: Zettai Kijō

Rime's answer to the question: what happens when a three-ton mech achieves full mount? The Rime Sphere tackles its target to the ground, and instead of transitioning to Sphere Mode for a Rolling Thunder follow-up, it stays in Walker Mode and establishes a crushing top position — all four reinforced legs pinning the opponent's limbs while the mech's dense central chassis presses down with gravitational authority. The technique borrows directly from Rime's personal BJJ vocabulary: mount, isolate, submit. From this position, Rime has three submission paths. The first is a thermal press — the chassis superheats to forge-temperature, cooking through armour. The second is a Ryou cascade — channelling destructive Haki through all four leg-joints simultaneously into the opponent's pressure points, overloading their nervous system. The third — and Rime's favourite — is the Electro ground-and-pound, where each hydraulic foreleg strikes with piston-driven force while discharging maximum voltage through the contact point. The rhythm mimics Rime's natural BJJ ground game scaled to siege proportions: patient, methodical, inescapable. The opponent cannot bridge out from under three tons. They cannot shrimp. They cannot frame. They can only endure until the mech decides to let them go, which is never, because Rime has never once voluntarily abandoned top position in any weight class. He considers this a point of personal pride.

Fujian White Crane ・ Xingyiquan ・ Tibetan White Crane ・ Wing Chun

The Silent Wing

Petal's pure fighting style is the most refined counter-fighting art on the Meridian. Drawing from four disciplines that share a philosophical core — patience, precision, and the devastating power of centerline control — she has synthesized a form uniquely suited to a small avian body with Mink-level physical capabilities and the world's most advanced Observation Haki. She does not initiate. She does not chase. She reads, she waits, and when the opening arrives, she ends the conversation in a single exchange.
